extern type Number;
cap Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self }
cap Mul { fn mul(a: Self, b: Self): Self }
extern fn js_add(a: Number, b: Number): Number;
extern fn js_mul(a: Number, b: Number): Number;
impl Number: Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self = resume(js_add(a, b)) }
impl Number: Mul { fn mul(a: Self, b: Self): Self = resume(js_mul(a, b)) }

fn inner(x: Number): Number = Add.add(x, x)
fn outer(x: Number): Number = Mul.mul(inner(x), inner(x))
fn main(): Number = outer(3)

===EXPECT===
# Multi-cap chain: resolving `outer` requires `Mul` + two nested calls to
# `inner`, which itself requires `Add`. Both caps have default impls, so
# dep_free's fixed-point iteration marks `inner` (leaf) first, then `outer`
# once `inner` is known dep-free. Both get optimized:
#   - `inner` is cloned as `inner__lto` (two call-sites from `outer`).
#   - `outer` is absorbed into `__main_cps` (single caller, body small).
# Key invariants in `__main_cps`'s body:
#   - direct `js_mul(a, b)` from `Mul.mul` inlining.
#   - `inner__lto(x)` called twice (the clone is used, not the original).
#   - no `__caps.Mul_Number` / `__caps.Add_Number` accesses in the body.
# The outer `main()` wrapper still constructs the cap bundle (same v1 floor
# issue as fixture 01/02) — assertions target `__main_cps` and `inner__lto`.
inner__lto
js_add(x, x)
__main_cps
js_mul(a, b)
inner__lto(x)
!__caps.Add_Number.add
!__caps.Mul_Number.mul
!outer__lto
